author: @russellzauner
text: if you had plates of different weights then you could theoretically make an automatic transmission based on manipulating the variables of torque related to speed.  I am trying to solve a few problems on my transportation - gearing is one; more importantly, I burned up my brakes making an unexpected 1500 ft descent in traffic on my "wheelchair" (recumbent tadpole with pedal assist) and am seeking to understand better how to make, well, brakes but not for energy (although the current probably needs to dump somewhere) and instead to save my mechanical brakes for lower speed/hold stop use.  If I can basically "speed limit" my downhill motion then I won't need to use much of the mechanical ones at all, until I have to come to and hold a stop.  Since I have two front wheels I can implement 2/3 braking power on each side and still end up with a surplus.  I would switch to trials brakes (basically rim brakes that have super high performance pads instead of disc, the rims can soak up and the rotation disperse more heat) but can't really mount them well because of the side hubs.  Dynos don't have any braking control; tiny hub motors would work but they are also heavy (I could have reverse then but I'd rather have a shiftable geartrain).  I would build something but there's not really any plans and I'd rather understand what I'm doing than just keep iterating blindly until something does a little something.

author: @christianburke418
text: I invented the SPHEREMOTOR. Wrap hundreds of feet of enameled copper wire around a small plastic bowl. Take two strong disc magnets and let them attract together squeezing the center of a pencil. Add something to fill the gaps between the two magnets. Secure each end of the pencil so that it can rotate, and the magnet portion rest inside the wired wrapped bowl. Take some 9-volt batteries clip them together. Sand the two ends of bowl enameled wire and secure one end of the wire to the negative on 9-volt batteries.  Now tap the end of the other wire to the positive on the batteries. Keep tapping and watch the powerful motion it creates.     Where it becomes cool, is when the magnet around the aluminum axle is a sphere with 600lbs of pull strength and the tolerances between the wire wrapped bowl are really, really close, the timing of the alternating tapping is perfect, that's when it is magic. Now add another bowl winding to generate current from the spinning of the 600lbs pull strength sphere magnet inside it and this becomes something that uses very little current but creates a large amount of current.   To see drawings, search for spheremotor.

author: @maxkennedy5073
text: Hmmm...the torque limitation seems to be good for the internal parts but what about the blades in high wind speeds?  Even with the gears "locked" because they can slip would the blades not rotate anyway and potentially exceed their strength limitations due to the centripetal force?  Maybe there's some part I'm not understanding properly?  Could potentially be overcome by a separate braking system I guess.

replies.author: @Naomi_Boyd
replies.text: A separate braking system would probably be necessary, but the torque limiter would still help because it works both ways.  When one side slips, it adds resistance to the other side and would, at the very least, reduce the rotation rate of the turbine in much the same way that a heavy load on the generator would.

replies.author: @kadmow
replies.text: Yes, it will avoid runaway generators protecting the electrics from bursting into flames, but destroy the rotor if it overtorques and decouples.  Noting that aerodynamic rotors TIP speed operate at 6 or 7 times windspeed (TSR) while a deag device oy operates at up to TSR = 1, much less likely to be catastrophic. (And a diffuser fwd device can easily be "throttled".)

author: @Cooliemasteroz
text: Magnetic gears may be useful in electric cars. The reason being is that you can have a high speed low torque electric motor which would be much lighter than a low speed high torque motor of the same power and the high speed motor would draw less current. Unfortunately the high gear ratios needed are not so practical with mechanical gears because of the noise but mainly because of the severe braking which could be overcome with magnetic gears that can be made to slip when needed more easily.

replies.author: @Greg_Chase
replies.text:
The high water friction requires encasing the moving magnet wheels etc. in non-ferrous materials to
1)  reduce/eliminate 'water drag' friction on the moving wheels
2)  non-ferrous to all the fields to only 'work on' the desired components, not the housing

You probably intended that, since the 'water drag' friction is an added drag not present in an out-of-water system

author: @MK-lk7nc
text: This is quite interesting, I've always been fascinated with magnet projects I see online and the various ways they seem to all be chasing the same unicorn.  One thing I've wondered about is a vertical arrangement of magnets attached to a floater aligned to a pole anchored into a coastal area so the waves and tides could drive the floater up and down, as a generator.  I'd love to see your version of such a device someday.   Btw have you ever tried printing in nylon?  I've found it has quite a few advantages over PLA/ABS for certain forms, if you can overcome it's tendency to distort a fair bit over the print height.  I've come up with some ways to mitigate that somewhat if you'd like I can try to elaborate on that more.  Short version is, you need a thermal co-efficient that varies per layer, since a heated bed creates base-layer bias.  Two substantial advantages to nylon for these sort of applications are it's profound strength, far in excess of pla/abs or almost anything short of HDPE.  Another is it's naturally lubricated nature of extremely low friction in the event parts touch.  Also nice is it's fairly non-toxic (medical implants and such are frequently made of medical grade nylon).  And it's layer adhesion is top notch, completely fused.

author: @jamest.5001
text: Maybe you could build a mag gear box with a low rpm. Generator,, usingva gear reduction,  with a over drive section. With a centrafugal clutch.  To allow a over drive when the wind is slow,  say 5-7 mph wind giving  up to 400 rpm,  then slightly over. The clutch, or band takes hold. And swaps ratios. Requring a 7-25 mph wind to spin from 500 to 900 rpm,  with a brake to stop, or slow the turbine around 1000rpm.. with tgd turbine spinning approximately 2000-2500rpm, so the tq  amplification should be decent,  using the magnets and coil specifics to get the current and voltage required,  harness all the power from the turbine without stalling it, also make it usefil in lower wind speeds, and not required such hi speed wind to start making power!  It may only be 50-100 watts at start,  however the TQ should  allow more than 20x that near the peak, on that example,  completely off the wall, zero math involved!   Or a turbine could be set up different, to allow the generator vto fpin fast, to make more power,  using gearing to regulate the voltage, keeping it steady, used like a magnetic governor.   The possibly is endless,  use 75mm x 3mm magnets, with iron shaped to get the flux air faps close as possible, to increase efficientcy  if needed depending on the shape of tge gear box,  naking a axial gear should not require any, a planetary can use either shaped magnets  ir iron to nake up the difference, ,   its an awesome idea, maybe a cvt can be designed, allowing the turbine to spin fast as it can efficiently, then gear the output suit the generator!

author: @tommycrist1856
text: Thank you. I tested out my darwin/tesla generator the other day. the coils melted down and caught fire because it was spinning to fast even with a load on it. Made for an interesting afternoon for sure. I'm now trying a 4 to 1 ratio larger/smaller disc size to see if this solves the problem. If it doesn't, I'll give this a try. Oh, and that was with an 8 MPH wind speed.

replies.author: @mikedodger7898
replies.text: Photos or video? Wow.

replies.author: @tommycrist1856
replies.text: @mikedodger7898 Sadly no. But thanks for asking. I was going to make a video IF it worked. But it would have made for a good one. I will tell you that the tesla discs were just some old 78rpm records on a shaft I had made with pool balls on the end. I'm using air those for air bearings. I had two discs with the magnets and coils on both sides of those with stationary coils. The darwin wind catcher as I call it was attached to a funnel attached to the housing for the discs. As I posted to Robert for another video of his, once it starts spinning it starts sucking in more air. I'm no expert on any of this. I just play with it to see if it works. I'm hoping that by reducing the tesla discs to the size of CDs, that it works better.

replies.author: @kadmow
replies.text: @tommycrist1856 , a reversed funnel on the outlet (bigger diameter than the inlet funnel) may give an "air multiplier" effect... You go, realworld testing beats guesses.

replies.author: @tommycrist1856
replies.text: @kadmow I'll see how this next build works. In my mind's eye, I think I will have to do away with the funnel altogether or make the opening smaller to restrict the airflow. I'm making it so that you can put it on your home, so strong winds will be a problem it looks like, as to where other wind powered one seek out higher wind speeds. And do this within a 36 inch tall structure.

author: @mikek.1761
text: Please, can you explain how the slippage will prevent runaway turbine? I believe that because of the slippage the turbine can get easily into runaway as there's nothing mechanical to brake the propeller. The generator acts with opposite force to the spin when engaged. If the magnetic coupling is broken even that opposite force is gone. Did I miss something?

replies.author: @BillHallProductions
replies.text:
I think he means, the gears won't be destoryed if you pass their limit.

A normal gear would strip and be permanently destroyed when overloaded

A magnetic gear might slip a few steps but it will keep working when the load is reduced 

I imagine it keeps providing drag no matter what

replies.author: @mikek.1761
replies.text: @BillHallProductions he was talking about safety feature. In runaway turbines the main concern is broken propeller flying around neighborhood and damaging stuff and/or people & animals. Broken gears are not that important comparing to health damage.

replies.author: @ShivaTD420
replies.text: When the gears are out of sync the slower one acts like a brake/clutch

Just a thought (suspect Rob would have a better idea on how to go about it! 😂 👍)
